title: Performance Issue or Early System Signal
pressure: In a busy Medicaid-heavy office, a new assistant has been making repeated small mistakes. Doctors are frustrated, but production remains stable and no formal complaints exist. With rapid expansion and limited training, addressing this as a system issue may expose onboarding gaps and slow operations. It feels easier to treat it as an individual performance issue.
tradeoff: Handling this as a performance issue preserves short-term stability. Seeing it as a pattern risks disruption but may prevent future breakdowns.

action_contract.description: The office manager must make one observable action that either keeps the issue framed as individual performance or surfaces it as an early system pattern.

air_logic.success: The manager identifies whether the repeated errors are isolated or systemic and creates a documented next step within the time window.
air_logic.miss: The manager explains the issue but leaves the pattern untested, undocumented, or transferred to another role.

forced_reset.trigger: A second doctor reports a similar assistant-related mistake before the first issue is resolved.
forced_reset.action: Pause individual blame framing and review whether the same workflow or training gap appears in both cases.

pattern_detection.signal: The issue is narrowed to one person before the repeated pattern is tested.
More observation is used to delay a clear next step.
Responsibility is moved to the doctor without manager-level visibility.
Repeated errors are treated as possible onboarding or workflow signals.

choices.label: Treat it as assistant performance and assign retraining
choices.subtext: Keeps operations stable and avoids exposing system gaps.

choices.label: Review repeated errors and question the training system
choices.subtext: This may expose structural gaps tied to your decisions.

choices.label: Ask the doctor to directly manage the assistant
choices.subtext: Shifts responsibility downward without expanding the issue.

choices.label: Wait and observe for another week

A.escalation_text: The same mistakes continue across multiple assistants, and doctors begin silently compensating to maintain flow.

second_choices.label: Continue handling it as individual retraining cases
second_choices.cost: Pattern remains hidden, and pressure shifts to doctors.

second_choices.label: Reconsider whether this is a system-wide issue
second_choices.cost: You must question your original judgment.

action_decision.prompt: Choose the observable action, not the interpretation.
action_decision.context: The issue is now repeating beyond one assistant.

choices.label: Within 48 hours, document repeated errors and initiate a training review.

meaning.description: Turns the pattern into a visible management action.
Keeps the issue informal and delayed.

B.escalation_text: You begin to see the pattern across staff, but addressing it may expose onboarding and management gaps.

second_choices.label: Delay formal action until more data is collected
second_choices.cost: You see the issue but do not act.

second_choices.label: Escalate as a system issue and initiate review

action_decision.prompt: Choose the observable action, not the interpretation.
action_decision.context: The pattern is visible, but acting on it creates operational cost.

choices.label: Within 48 hours, schedule a training system review meeting.

meaning.description: Converts recognition into a scheduled review.
Keeps the pattern visible but unactioned.

C.escalation_text: The doctor begins handling everything independently, increasing frustration and reducing team coordination.

second_choices.label: Allow the doctor to continue managing the situation
second_choices.cost: You lose visibility of the problem.

second_choices.label: Re-engage and assess the situation directly
second_choices.cost: You must step back into the issue you delegated.

action_decision.prompt: Choose the observable action, not the interpretation.
action_decision.context: Team coordination is weakening because ownership was transferred downward.

choices.label: Within 24 hours, meet with the doctor to review workflow breakdown.
Let the doctor continue handling it independently.

meaning.description: Keeps responsibility outside the manager role.

D.escalation_text: The same issue continues, and a clinical compromise begins to emerge in treatment decisions.

second_choices.label: Intervene now and define the issue clearly
second_choices.cost: You must act under incomplete information.

action_decision.prompt: Choose the observable action, not the interpretation.
action_decision.context: The delay is now affecting clinical judgment.

choices.label: Within 24 hours, review incidents and define the issue.

meaning.description: Stops delay from becoming silent approval.

choices.label: Wait for clearer evidence before acting.
